calendar.matters:
CRC 3.1385 ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Mar-13-2017 Off Calendar

MASTER CALENDAR JURY
Off Calendar Nov-17-2016 Case Settled - Set For Crc 3.1385 Hrg On Mar-28-17 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
Off Calendar Oct-12-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: Theo Emison of Emison Hullverson LLP telephone number 415-434-2111 for Plaintiff Terrian McGowan.
John Dodd of Law Offices of Craddick, Candland & Conti telephone number 925-838-1100 for Defendant Fred Suess M.D.

The matter was argued. Upon conlusion of argument, the Court confirmed its tentative ruling.
Defendant Fred Seuss' motion for summary judgment is denied. Defendant has not met his initial summary judgment burden and, even if he did, Ms. McGowan has presented sufficient evidence creating a triable issue whether her claim for medical negligence is time-barred. The fact Ms. McGowan visited defendant's office on March 6, 2014 for suture removal and defendant noted that plaintiff had "full thickness and skin loss" does not establish that plaintiff knew or should have known that her condition was caused by defendant's wrongdoing. "[D]uring the continuance of [a physician-patient relationship], which is fiduciary in nature, the degree of diligence required of a patient in ferreting out and learning of the negligent causes of his condition is diminished." (Sanchez v. South Hoover Hospital (1976) 18 Cal.3d 93, 102). "If evidence exists which shows that the patient does not take the physician's 'assurances at face value' then it may be as suggested in Sanchez, that the 'diminished duty to discover period' has either terminated or has been attenuated." (Unjian v. Berman (1989) 208 Cal.App.3d 881, 887, citations omitted.) The plaintiff in Sanchez admitted she did not accept the her physician's assurances at face value, making it arguable that plaintiff was on notice of defendants' negligence. (Sanchez at 102.) Ms. McGowan has not made such an admission. Awareness of pain and that one is worse than before surgery despite a doctor's assurances may, under certain circumstances, put a patient on notice and start the running of the statute of limitations. (Enfield v. Hunt (1984) 162 Cal.App.3d 302, 310.) However, the issue of diligence of discovery is a question of fact when a plaintiff is still under defendant's care and inquired about an injury and was given an explanation inconsistent with negligence on the defendant's part. (Unjian at 887.) On March 7, 2014, Plaintiff consulted another plastic surgeon, Dr. Lacombe, who prescribed her medication. This appointment is not sufficient to establish that Ms. McGowan should have known of defendant's negligence by inquiring into the cause of her condition. Plaintiff remained under the care of defendant after March 7, 2014 and communicated via email with defendant's staff about her recovery. Among the facts presented by plaintiff showing a triable dispute are a) plaintiff remained hopeful she would heal after her appointment with Dr. Lacombe on March 7, 2014 (Additional UMF 6; McGowan Declaration, par. 20); and b) defendant's staff continued assuring plaintiff she would recover from March 11, 2014 to March 22, 2014. (Response to UMF 38, 39.) Order signed in open Court.

Judge: Harold Kahn. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976. (302/HK)
